data.title: Johnson & Johnson Therapy Delivers Strong Results In Colorectal Cancer Trial
data.description:
On Saturday, Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) shared new longer follow-up results from the Phase 1b/2 OrigAMI-1 study.

The trial is evaluating Rybrevant (amivantamab-vmjw) in combination with FOLFOX or FOLFIRI chemotherapy for RAS/BRAF wild-type metastatic colorectal cancer.

The company said that encouraging anti-tumor activity, durable responses, and low rates of treatment-related discontinuations observed in this study support further investigation in ongoing Phase 3 studies in first- and second-line colorectal cancer.

Results were presented at the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology Gastrointestinal Cancers Symposium.

Detailed Study Results

At a median follow-up of 16 months, amivantamab plus FOLFOX (n=20) or FOLFIRI (n=23) achieved a confirmed overall response rate (ORR) of 51% across the study, with responses observed early and a median time to first response of 8.3 weeks, along with a median DOR of 9.3 months.

Median PFS was 9.2 months.

In the first-line subgroup, ORR was 73%, with a median DOR not yet reached at the time of data cutoff.

Among 11 patients treated in the first-line subgroup, four were able to proceed to curative intent surgery. In the second-line subgroup (n=32), ORR was 44% and median DOR was 7.4 months.

More than one-third of patients treated in the second-line setting remained on therapy for over one year, and three patients have stayed on amivantamab treatment for more than two years.

In patients with liver metastases (n=30), the study showed notable activity, with an ORR of 57% and a median PFS of 11.3 months.

The safety profile remained consistent with prior reports of amivantamab plus chemotherapy in colorectal cancer and with the known safety profiles of the individual agents.

Broader Pipeline and Regulatory Updates

Last week, Johnson & Johnson said topline results from the Phase 2b JASMINE study of nipocalimab in lupus patients met the primary endpoint, and key secondary and exploratory endpoints.

In December 2025, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved Rybrevant Faspro (amivantamab and hyaluronidase-lpuj), the first and only subcutaneously administered therapy for epidermal growth factor receptor-mutated non-small cell lung cancer.

Rybrevant Faspro is approved for all indications for which Rybrevant is approved.

JNJ Price Action: Johnson & Johnson shares were up 1.61% at $207.69 at the time of publication on Monday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo by Aunt Spray via Shutterstock

data.title: Gold, Silver Spike To Record Highs As Fed Independence Falters: What's Moving Markets Monday?
data.description:
Gold and silver surged to fresh record highs Monday as investors reassessed the Federal Reserve's independence fears amid a new political escalation.

• MACOM Technology stok is approaching key resistance levels. What’s behind MTSI new highs?

Gold prices — as tracked by the SPDR Gold Shares (NYSE:GLD) — jumped 2.5% to $4,620 an ounce, setting another all-time high. Silver also soared more than 7% to $86 an ounce, extending its 12-month rally to over 190%

The move followed reports that the Department of Justice opened a criminal probe into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell over renovation costs at the Fed's headquarters.

Powell framed the investigation as a pretext to pressure the central bank into cutting interest rates, directly clashing with President Donald Trump's policy demands.

Markets read the development as another attempt by the administration to challenge the Fed's independence, with investors selling Treasuries and rotating aggressively into precious metals.

Major stock averages on Wall Street showed timid gains as the market adopted a wait-and-see stance ahead of a busy week of macro data and earnings. On Tuesday, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will release December inflation data.

The fourth-quarter earnings season also kicks off, with JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM), Bank of New York Mellon Corp. (NYSE:BK) and Delta Air Lines Inc. (NYSE:DAL) reporting before the opening bell.

Financial stocks underperformed on Monday after Trump proposed capping credit card interest rates at 10%. Capital One Financial Corp. (NYSE:COF) tumbled 6.6%, posting its worst session since April 10, 2025. American Express Co. (NYSE:AXP) fell nearly 5%, while Citigroup Inc. (NYSE:C) dropped 3.5%.

In contrast, Vistra Corp. (NYSE:VST) led the S&P 500 higher, gaining more than 6% after Friday's 10.5% surge. Shares continued to climb on optimism surrounding a multi-year power contract with Meta Platforms Inc. (NASDAQ:META).

Oil prices were little changed at $59 a barrel, shrugging off geopolitical turmoil in Iran. Bitcoin (CRYPTO: BTC) rebounded 1.5% to $92,000.

Monday's Performance In Major U.S. Indices, ETFs

According to Benzinga Pro data:

  • The Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (NYSE:VOO) flattened at $638.74.
  • The SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average (NYSE:DIA) moved -0.16% to $494.25.
  • The tech-heavy Invesco QQQ Trust Series (NASDAQ:QQQ) eased 0.12% to $627.38.
  • The iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSE:IWM) traded at $260.86.
  • The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLP) outperformed, up 1.1%; the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSE:XLF) lagged, down 1.0%.

Russell 1000's Top 5 Gainers And Losers On Monday

data.title: Chipotle CEO Says Gen Z And Millennials 'Feel The Pinch' And Cooking At Home—'We're Not Losing Them To The Competition…We're Losing Them To Grocery'
data.description:
There's a new kind of ghosting happening in the restaurant world—and it has nothing to do with taste. According to Chipotle CEO Scott Boatwright, younger customers aren't abandoning burritos for rival chains. They're abandoning restaurants altogether.

Speaking during the company's October earnings call, Boatwright laid out the shift plainly. "We're not losing them to the competition," he said. "We're losing them to grocery and food at home."

That change is most visible among diners ages 25 to 35, a group that accounts for roughly 25% of Chipotle's customer base. Boatwright said those customers are facing a combination of unemployment pressure, student loan repayment, and slower real wage growth—factors that make eating out an easy expense to cut.

Don't Miss:

  • The ‘ChatGPT of Marketing' Just Opened a $0.85/Share Round — 10,000+ Investors Are Already In
  • Missed the AI Boom's Biggest IPOs? This Platform Lets Everyday Investors Access Private Tech Early

"They feel the pinch," Boatwright said. "We feel the pullback from them as well."

The pressure doesn't stop there. Boatwright also pointed to households earning under $100,000 a year, which make up about 40% of Chipotle's sales. That group, he said, has pulled back on dining frequency even more sharply, contributing to what he described as a broad slowdown in transactions.

What's notable is what he did not say. This isn't about fading brand loyalty. "It shows we are gaining market share," Boatwright told analysts. In other words, Chipotle isn't losing customers to Taco Bell or Shake Shack—it's losing visits to tighter household budgets.

That distinction matters because it points to a bigger shift in the economy. For years, a $100,000 household income was treated as a kind of financial finish line. Today, it increasingly sits in a gray zone—above the median income, but far from insulated.

Trending: Americans With a Financial Plan Can 4X Their Wealth — Get Your Personalized Plan from a CFP Pro

According to the Income Paradox Survey — November 2025, conducted by The Harris Poll, even higher earners are cutting back on basics. Among households earning $100,000 or more annually, 41% say they are either currently skipping meals or planning to do so to make ends meet.

That helps explain why grocery stores are winning share from restaurants. Even households that look comfortable on paper are reassessing everyday spending, swapping convenience for control and dining out for meals at home. Chipotle may still be cheaper than many fast‑casual peers, but when budgets tighten, the comparison isn't restaurant versus restaurant—it's restaurant versus groceries.

Boatwright acknowledged the challenge, describing "a broad-based pullback in frequency" and "slowing transaction trends" across the business. Still, he made clear the company won't chase traffic with discounts. "Value as a price point is not and will not be a Chipotle strategy," he said.

Instead, Chipotle plans to focus on execution, menu innovation, and communicating value more clearly. Boatwright noted that Chipotle's pricing has tracked closer to food at home than food away from home, and that its meals remain 20–30% cheaper than many peers. The strategy is to stay positioned as an affordable option—even as consumers rethink what "affordable" really means.

For households earning around or above $100,000, that reassessment is becoming more common. Income alone no longer guarantees breathing room, especially with housing, food, and debt costs eating up a larger share of paychecks.

But this is where planning matters. For professionals and households earning $100,000 or more, Domain Money offers personalized financial planning led by lprofessionals who help people navigate exactly these trade‑offs. Through free strategy sessions, Domain helps clients look beyond salary headlines and build plans around cash flow, spending priorities, and long‑term goals—so short‑term pullbacks don't turn into long‑term stress.

Boatwright framed the moment as one to refocus rather than retreat. The company, he said, is working to "get back to consistent share gains" as conditions stabilize.

But the takeaway reaches far beyond burritos. When even six‑figure households are skipping meals and cooking more at home, it's a signal that the economy's pressure points have shifted. And right now, the toughest competition for restaurants isn't another chain—it's the household budget itself.

Image: Imagn
